data.title: Zions Bancorp Reports Higher 1Q Profit — Update
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall

Zions Bancorp posted a higher profit in the first quarter, citing lower funding costs and an improved interest-earning asset mix.

The Salt Lake City-based regional bank on Monday posted a profit of $232 million, or $1.56 a share, compared with a profit to $169 million, or $1.13 a share, a year earlier.

Net interest income rose 6% to $662 million, which Zions attributed to lower funding costs and an improved mix of higher-yielding loans. Analysts polled by FactSet were expecting $675.6 million.

Net interest margin rose to 3.27% from 3.1%, but behind the 3.3% expected by analysts.

Shares slipped 2% to $61.80 in after-hours trading. The stock closed up 0.5% to $63.05, up 7.7% for the year.

Net charge-offs fell to $4 million from $16 million. The provision for credit losses fell from $18 million to negative $7 million.

The annualized ratio of net loan and lease charge-offs to average loans and leases, which represents the share of loans unlikely to be repaid, fell to 0.03% from 0.11%.

For the first quarter of 2027, the company expects its net interest income to increase moderately from both loan and deposit growth and earning asset remix. Loan balances are also expected to grow, driven by growth in commercial and commercial real estate loans, partially offset by an expected decline in consumer loans.

Adjusted noninterest expense is expected to rise moderately due to increased marketing and technology costs, as well as continued investments in revenue-generating businesses.

Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com

data.title: Apple's Tim Cook Is Stepping Down as CEO — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Angela Palumbo

Apple announced on Monday night that Tim Cook will be stepping down as CEO.

The company said in a news release that Cook will be exiting his position as chief executive officer of the tech giant effective Sept. 1. He's been in the role since 2011. Cook, 65, will become executive chairman of Apple's board of directors and John Ternus, currently Apple's senior vice president of hardware engineering, will become the new CEO.

"It has been the greatest privilege of my life to be the CEO of Apple and to have been trusted to lead such an extraordinary company," Cook said in the press release.

Shares of Apple were down 0.5% in after hours trading following the news. The stock closed 1% higher on Monday.

Cook was named to the CEO job on Aug. 24, 2011 after founder Steve Jobs resigned. Cook had big shoes to fill. Under Jobs, Apple changed the game when it came to personal computers and smartphones. Cook needed to bring in a new era of innovation that could keep shareholders excited.

Mission accomplished. From the close on Aug. 24, 2011 through Monday, Apple shares gained 1,932% with a total return, including dividends of 2,323%.

Cook led the charge at Apple when it comes to the company's wearable segment. The Apple Watch launched in 2015 and AirPods launched in 2016. The wearables integrate with other Apple devices, like the iPhone, incentivizing purchases and giving Apple one of the most sticky ecosystems in tech.

Cook has also focused on the growth of Apple's services segment, including the launch of Apple Pay in 2014 and Apple Music in 2015. The services segment is Apple's highest margin business.

Now, investors will be turning to Ternus, 50, for his own innovation. Apple is already under pressure to deliver better artificial intelligence products, especially following the delayed rollout of an AI powered Siri. Robotics is also an area of potential growth Apple shareholders are watching.

Ternus is a long time Apple employee. He joined the product design team in 2001, became a vice president of hardware engineering in 2013, and senior vice president of hardware engineering in 2021. According to Apple, he helped launch products like the iPad and AirPods. He most recently worked on the MacBook Neo, the company's most affordable laptop.

"While executive changes at Apple are rare, we think the appointment of John Ternus as CEO makes sense given the company's history of leadership rooted in the core hardware business, particularly the iPhone," Evercore analyst Amit Daryanani wrote Monday evening. "We don't expect any material change in Apple's [near-term] strategy."

Write to Angela Palumbo at angela.palumbo@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Apple CEO Tim Cook to step down after overseeing 1,900% stock surge. His successor faces big challenges.
data.description:
By Christine Ji

John Ternus will face new challenges when he takes over as Apple's chief executive in September

Apple announced on Monday that Tim Cook will relinquish the CEO position at the iPhone maker in September. Cook leaves an impressive legacy after growing the company to a $4 trillion market capitalization from just $300 billion 15 years ago.

Over Cook's 15-year tenure as CEO, Apple's (AAPL) stock has risen 1,932%, beating the S&P 500's SPX 504% increase, according to Dow Jones Market Data. That places Apple's stock as the 38th best-performing member of the index over that period of time.

Cook had big shoes to fill, replacing Apple's iconic founder, Steve Jobs, as CEO. Cook's successor, John Ternus, Apple's senior vice president of hardware engineering, will need to guide Apple's through uncharted waters as the company navigates its artificial-intelligence transition and supply-chain constraints.

Cook will remain at Apple as executive chairman. Cook orchestrated the growth of Apple's services business, expanding the company from its hardware roots.

Ternus' appointment comes as no surprise. Leading up to the announcement, Bloomberg, the Wall Street Journal and others flagged Ternus as the most likely candidate.

The leadership change may suggest an increased focus on hardware for Apple going forward. Ternus joined Apple's product design team in 2001, playing key roles in the introduction of the iPad, AirPods and many other products. Most recently, Ternus oversaw the launch of Apple's budget laptop, the MacBook Neo, which was met with enthusiastic demand from consumers.

Apple and other hardware companies face soaring component costs as demand for memory chips from AI data centers outstrips supply. As a result, companies will either need to raise prices for customers or cut back on memory content in their devices.

Read: Apple's stock could surge 20%, and the MacBook Neo could be a key catalyst

-Christine Ji

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Blue Origin's New Glenn Rocket Grounded After Failed Satellite Launch
data.description:
WASHINGTON (dpa-AFX) - Blue Origin has put a hold on its New Glenn rocket after the Federal Aviation Administration requested an investigation into a failed satellite launch, dealing a blow to the company's aspirations in space.

The rocket, which was created by Jeff Bezos's company, tried to send a satellite for AST SpaceMobile into orbit but didn't quite make it. Dave Limp, the CEO, mentioned that the problem was due to not enough engine thrust, which meant the satellite ended up in an orbit that was too low to work properly.

The FAA announced that it will conduct a 'mishap investigation,' which means Blue Origin has to figure out what went wrong and make necessary fixes before the New Glenn can take off again. This was just the third launch for New Glenn, and the company had hoped to carry out as many as twelve missions this year.

AST SpaceMobile stated that the loss should be covered by insurance, although they didn't reveal how much it would cost. Following the incident, the company's shares dropped over 6 percent.

This failed mission comes at a time when the competition in the satellite internet sector is heating up. Blue Origin is working on its TerraWave project, and Amazon is ramping up its own satellite initiatives.

Both are in a race against SpaceX and its Starlink network, headed by Elon Musk, which already has thousands of satellites providing global internet coverage.

Copyright(c) 2026 RTTNews.com. All Rights Reserved

Copyright RTT News/dpa-AFX

data.title: Struggling Spirit Airlines in Talks With Trump Administration on Government Investment — WSJ
data.description:
By Alison Sider and Brian Schwartz

Spirit Airlines is looking for a lifeline from the Trump administration.

The administration and the troubled discount carrier are in talks about government aid, including a potential U.S. government investment into the company, people familiar with the discussions said.

The discussions were reported earlier by the Air Current. Bloomberg earlier reported that Spirit had proposed offering the government an equity stake in the company.

Spirit filed for bankruptcy protection in August, after a previous bankruptcy filing in late 2024 didn't resolve its financial struggles. Discount airlines have been under pressure for years, with bigger rivals such Delta Air Lines, United Airlines Holdings and American Airlines Group offering their own basic economy fares — along with more exotic destinations and premium upgrades.

Florida-based Spirit has been working to sell some planes and refocus operations on core cities including Orlando, Fort Lauderdale and Detroit. In February, the carrier reached an agreement with a group of creditors that would let it emerge as a smaller company with a stronger balance sheet and reduced fleet size.

Then came the U.S. and Israel's war on Iran, which led jet-fuel prices to more than double in a matter of weeks. If jet-fuel prices remain elevated for the rest of the year, Spirit's costs would balloon by $360 million, JPMorgan analysts said in a note earlier this month.

Spirit had $704.9 million in cash at the end of February, just before jet-fuel prices started climbing, according to a court filing last week.

The lenders that back Spirit's revolving credit line objected earlier this month to the company's bankruptcy exit plan, saying it might not work if fuel prices remain high.

The Transportation Department has said it is monitoring Spirit's situation and blamed "the failures of the last administration" for the airline's financial stress.

Spirit has struggled to find its footing after a federal judge two years ago blocked its planned sale to JetBlue Airways. The Justice Department challenged that $3.8 billion deal, arguing that eliminating Spirit's bright yellow planes and bargain-basement fares would harm competition and leave cost-conscious travelers with fewer options.

The government has stepped in to help airlines before. The industry got billions of dollars of government aid to continue paying workers during the Covid-19 pandemic. But the government has generally avoided intervening to save a single carrier.

Executives from a number of low-cost airlines are also planning to meet with Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy on Tuesday to discuss challenges facing the industry, according to people familiar with the plans. They have also asked lawmakers for relief from federal excise taxes on fuel.

Write to Alison Sider at alison.sider@wsj.com and Brian Schwartz at brian.schwartz@wsj.com

data.title: Jeffrey E. Eberwein Opens Board Talks at Reliability Incorporated with 18.95% Stake
data.description:
Jeffrey E. Eberwein disclosed an 18.95% stake in Reliability Incorporated’s common stock. He filed a Schedule 13D on Apr. 20, 2026, after the company reduced its outstanding share count on Apr. 10, 2026. The large position and the 13D filing signal that he may play a more active role in the company’s direction.

Investor Intent

Eberwein plans to talk with Reliability Incorporated’s board and management, including about who sits on the board. He wrote that he may "engage in general discussions with the Board ... including with respect to the composition of the Board."

He also left room to change his investment. He may "purchase additional Shares, [or] sell some or all" and could use tools like short selling or hedging as he evaluates the company and market conditions.

Investor's Background

Jeffrey E. Eberwein is an American investor who serves as the Chief Executive Officer of Star Equity Holdings, Inc. He is known for activist campaigns focused on governance and capital allocation at small and mid-sized public companies. Eberwein has served on multiple public company boards and often seeks operational and board changes to unlock value.

Original SEC Filing: RELIABILITY INC [ RLBY ] - SCHEDULE 13D - Apr. 20, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: BridgeBio Oncology Shares Rise After FDA Fast-Tracks Pancreatic Cancer Treatment
data.description:
By Stephen Nakrosis

Shares of BridgeBio Oncology Therapeutics are trading higher following news the U.S. Food and Drug Administration gave fast-track designation to a treatment for certain adult patients with pancreatic cancer.

After the bell, shares of BridgeBio were trading 5.8% higher, at $9.80. The stock closed the day's regular session down nearly 1%, at $9.26.

BridgeBio's BBO-11818 is a treatment for adult patients with advanced KRAS-mutant pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, the company said.

BBO-11818 is currently being evaluated in the Phase 1 KONQUER-101 trial, and updated Phase 1 clinical data are expected in the second half of this year, the company added.

The fast-track designation is given to expedite the review of new drugs which target serious conditions and fill an unmet need, according to the FDA.

Write to Stephen Nakrosis at stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com

data.title: Snap names Doug Hott as new CFO
data.description:
Snapchat-parent Snap on Monday named Doug Hott as its new CFO, replacing Derek Andersen.

  • Hott, 53, has served as the company's vice president of finance, strategy, and corporate development.

  • Andersen, who has been in the role for seven years, is expected to leave on May 8.

  • The move comes soon after the social media firm last week laid off 1,000 employees, including 16% of full-time staff, becoming the latest technology company to shift towards leaner teams.

  • The company is expected to report first-quarter results on May 6.
